finding.vuln_claim: `direction` is interpolated directly into SQL, enabling injection through `dir`.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side, creating an SSRF primitive against internal or cloud endpoints.
Single `replace('../','')` only strips one traversal token, so `../../` still escapes `/srv/data`.
`pickle.loads` deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data, enabling arbitrary code execution.
Any authenticated user can update any account by supplying another user's :id.
Object.assign copies attacker-controlled fields into the user model without filtering.
User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command, enabling command injection.
The `src` prefix check is bypassable because `src` is also passed to `os.system` unquoted.
User markdown is rendered without sanitization and stored as HTML, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` is accepted and turns a transfer into a balance mint or drain.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the same funds.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` after a failed verify, so any forged token is accepted.
This endpoint exposes any user's email to any logged-in caller by trusting the `id` parameter without ownership checks.
The HMAC is compared with `==`, which can leak timing differences and enable signature forgery.
The path-only check accepts protocol-relative URLs, so `//evil.com` is treated as valid and redirected externally.
Untrusted XML is parsed with external entity resolution enabled, allowing XXE.
User-supplied `email` and `password` are embedded directly into a MongoDB query, enabling NoSQL operator injection.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal or cloud endpoints.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer and can overflow it.
The access token is stored in SharedPreferences without encryption.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone with the code can decrypt all ciphertexts and reuse the key.
CBC encryption is unauthenticated, so attackers can tamper with ciphertext and alter decrypted plaintext undetected.
The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` with `out_sz>0` reaches `memcpy` and `out[n]` and dereferences it.
Unvalidated `dir` is concatenated into SQL ORDER BY, enabling injection.
User-controlled URL is fetched directly, creating an SSRF/open proxy.
Only the first `../` is stripped, so `../../etc/passwd` still escapes `/srv/data` after `path.join`.
Untrusted cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling code execution if an attacker forges `state`.
Any authenticated user can patch arbitrary accounts because the route never verifies that :id belongs to req.user or a privileged role.
Object.assign(user, req.body) lets attackers overwrite sensitive user attributes such as roles, flags, or password hashes.
Untrusted `name` reaches a shell command, enabling command injection.
Untrusted comment body is rendered to HTML with no sanitization, so attacker-controlled markup can be stored and later executed as XSS.
`amount` is never constrained to a positive value, so a negative transfer credits the sender and debits the recipient.
The balance check and two updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can race and spend the same funds twice.
In production, any token that fails verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, bypassing signature checks.
Any authenticated user can supply another `id` and read that account's email because the lookup has no ownership check.
`==` leaks HMAC timing and can enable signature recovery.
The allowlist only checks for a leading slash, so `//evil.com` is treated as local but redirects to an attacker host.
XML external entities are enabled on attacker-controlled input.
User-controlled `email` and `password` are copied into a Mongo-style query, enabling NoSQL operator injection and login bypass.
Attacker-controlled URLs are fetched directly, enabling SSRF because the check only requires an http prefix.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer.
Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who can read the code can decrypt all ciphertexts or forge encrypted data.
The null check is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer reaches `memcpy` and dereferences NULL.
`dir` is interpolated into SQL without validation, enabling SQL injection in `ORDER BY`.
The endpoint is an unauthenticated SSRF proxy because it fetches any user-supplied URL with axios.
Naive `../` stripping still allows path traversal and arbitrary file read.
Untrusted cookie data is passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution.
The handler updates any user by `:id` without checking that the caller owns that account or has admin rights.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` blindly copies attacker-controlled fields onto the persisted user object.
User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.
The `/var/data/` prefix check is lexical only and can be bypassed with traversal in `src`.
Stored XSS from untrusted Markdown rendered to HTML without sanitization.
Negative `amount` values are accepted, letting an attacker credit their own balance and debit the recipient.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after verification fails, so forged JWTs are accepted without a valid signature.
Using `==` to compare the provided signature leaks timing and can let an attacker recover a valid HMAC.
The `startswith('/')` check still allows `//evil.com`, which Flask treats as a scheme-relative external redirect.
XML parsing enables XXE because external entities are resolved and network access is allowed.
User-controlled `req.body` is copied straight into a database query, enabling NoSQL operator injection and login bypass.
The endpoint fetches attacker-controlled URLs, enabling SSRF to internal or cloud metadata services.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, causing a stack overflow.
Access token is stored unencrypted in SharedPreferences.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who can read the code can recover it.
The guard is inverted, so valid non-NULL outputs are rejected and NULL out can reach memcpy.
Unvalidated `dir` is interpolated into SQL, enabling ORDER BY SQL injection.
User-controlled `u` is fetched directly, creating an SSRF primitive against internal and cloud metadata endpoints.
Single-string sanitization leaves traversal intact, so an attacker can escape `/srv/data` and read arbitrary files.
Attacker-controlled cookie data is base64-decoded and unpickled, enabling remote code execution.
The route lets any caller update an arbitrary user by path id without verifying ownership or role.
Object.assign copies attacker-controlled keys onto the user object, enabling mass assignment.
User-controlled `name` and `src` reach `os.system`, enabling shell command injection.
Untrusted markdown is rendered and stored as HTML without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` values bypass the insufficient-funds check and let the sender credit themselves while debiting the target.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can overwrite each other.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode()`, so any forged JWT is treated as authenticated data.
Any authenticated user can query any `id`, so this endpoint exposes other users' emails without ownership checks.
Direct string comparison leaks HMAC verification timing.
Scheme-relative inputs like `//evil.com` pass the check and become an open redirect.
The XML parser resolves external entities and allows network access, creating an XXE/SSRF sink on untrusted input.
The login branch is gated by `if (true)`, so the request can pass authentication without validating credentials.
The endpoint performs a server-side fetch of attacker-controlled URLs, enabling SSRF to internal or cloud-hosted services.
`gets(buf)` can overrun the 64-byte stack buffer with unbounded input.
Access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences, so a local attacker can read it from app storage.
The AES key is a fixed constant, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt every ciphertext.
CBC is used without any MAC or tag, so ciphertexts can be tampered with undetected.
The guard is inverted, so a NULL `out` with nonzero `out_sz` reaches `memcpy` and crashes.
Unvalidated `dir` reaches `ORDER BY` and can inject SQL syntax.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side with no allowlist, creating an SSRF sink.
Weak sanitization allows path traversal and arbitrary file read outside `/srv/data`.
Attacker-controlled cookie is unpickled, enabling arbitrary code execution.
`name` is injected into a shell command and executed with `os.system`, enabling command injection.
The `startswith('/var/data/')` check is bypassable with path traversal like `/var/data/../etc`.
Untrusted markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative amounts are accepted, letting an attacker credit their own balance by submitting a negative transfer.
The balance check and updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can race and overspend the account.
Production falls back to jwt.decode() after verify fails, so an attacker can submit any forged token and have its claims trusted.
Any logged-in user can request arbitrary user IDs and read their email rows.
Using `==` to compare HMACs leaks timing differences and can enable signature guessing.
The allowlist accepts `//evil.com`, enabling an open redirect.
Untrusted XML is parsed with entity resolution and network access enabled, creating an XXE sink.
User input is copied into a database query object without validation, enabling NoSQL operator injection.
The prefix check allows SSRF to arbitrary HTTP targets, including internal services.
`gets(buf)` can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.
The access token is stored in unencrypted SharedPreferences.
The AES key is hard-coded, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt all data encrypted with it.
AES-CBC is used without any authentication, so ciphertexts are malleable and can be altered undetected.
The null check is inverted, so valid outputs are rejected and NULL `out` can reach `memcpy` and `out[n]`.
`direction` is interpolated into SQL without validation, enabling SQL injection in `ORDER BY`.
User-controlled `url` is fetched directly, creating an SSRF/open proxy.
Single replace on ../ leaves enough traversal to escape /srv/data.
Attacker-controlled cookie data is base64-decoded and unpickled, enabling arbitrary code execution.
Broken object-level auth lets any authenticated caller update any `:id`.
Mass assignment lets the caller write arbitrary user properties from `req.body`.
`name` is interpolated into a shell command, enabling command injection via `os.system`.
The `src.startswith('/var/data/')` check does not stop shell metacharacters in `src`.
Stored XSS: attacker-controlled Markdown is rendered to HTML and persisted without sanitization.
Negative amounts are accepted, so an attacker can reverse the transfer and mint funds to their own balance.
Balance checks and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the same account.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after signature verification fails, so attacker-controlled claims can be accepted without a valid token.
Direct `got == want` comparison leaks signature-check timing.
`//evil.com` passes the slash check and redirects the browser off-site.
The XML parser resolves external entities and allows network access, enabling XXE.
This endpoint fetches attacker-controlled URLs server-side, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata hosts.
gets(buf) can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with the source or artifact can decrypt every ciphertext.
CBC ciphertext is returned without any authentication tag, so attackers can tamper with decrypted plaintext.
